examples.input: n=5, operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), find(0), find(3), union(1,2), find(0)
examples.output: After union(0,1): parent=[0,0,2,3,4]; After union(2,3): parent=[0,0,2,2,4]; find(0)=0; find(3)=2; After union(1,2): parent=[2,2,2,2,4]; find(0)=2
examples.explanation: Initialize parent=[0,1,2,3,4]. Union(0,1): find(0)=0, find(1)=1, rx=0, ry=1, update all elements with parent=1 to 0: parent[1]=0, parent=[0,0,2,3,4]. Union(2,3): find(2)=2, find(3)=3, rx=2, ry=3, update parent[3]=2, parent=[0,0,2,2,4]. Find(0): parent[0]=0, return 0. Find(3): parent[3]=2, parent[2]=2, return 2. Union(1,2): find(1)=0, find(2)=2, rx=0, ry=2, update all elements with parent=2 to 0: parent[2]=0, parent[3]=0, parent=[0,0,0,0,4]. Find(0): parent[0]=0, return 0.

examples.input: n=4, operations: union(0,2), union(1,3), find(2), union(0,3), find(1)
examples.output: After union(0,2): parent=[0,1,0,3]; After union(1,3): parent=[0,1,0,1]; find(2)=0; After union(0,3): parent=[1,1,1,1]; find(1)=1
examples.explanation: Initialize parent=[0,1,2,3]. Union(0,2): find(0)=0, find(2)=2, rx=0, ry=2, update parent[2]=0, parent=[0,1,0,3]. Union(1,3): find(1)=1, find(3)=3, rx=1, ry=3, update parent[3]=1, parent=[0,1,0,1]. Find(2): parent[2]=0, parent[0]=0, return 0. Union(0,3): find(0)=0, find(3)=1, rx=0, ry=1, update all elements with parent=1 to 0: parent[1]=0, parent[3]=0, parent=[0,0,0,0]. Find(1): parent[1]=0, parent[0]=0, return 0.

approaches.name: Naive Array DSU
approaches.summary: Maintain an array where each element stores its parent directly. For union operations, update all elements in one set to point to the other set's representative. This approach is straightforward but inefficient for large datasets.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Naive
ladders.desc: Implement a complete union-find data structure using the naive array representation with union, find, and connectivity operations.
ladders.explanation: The naive array representation of union-find maintains an array where each element stores its parent directly. Initially, each element is its own parent (self-loop). The find operation follows parent pointers until reaching a self-loop. The union operation merges two sets by updating all elements in one set to point to the representative of the other set. This approach is intuitive but inefficient for large datasets due to O(n) union operations. The intuition is to model disjoint sets as trees where each node points to its parent, and sets are merged by redirecting all nodes in one tree to the root of another. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize parent array of size n with parent[i] = i. 2. Find(x): while parent[x] != x: x = parent[x]; return x. 3. Union(x, y): find representatives rx and ry; if rx != ry: for i in range(n): if parent[i] == ry: parent[i] = rx. Time: O(n) per union, O(n) per find worst-case, O(1) per initialization. Space: O(n). This feeds into the full problem by providing a complete working solution. Common mistakes include not handling self-loops in find, incorrect union updates, and off-by-one errors in array indices. Real code pattern: class DSU: def __init__(self, n): self.parent = list(range(n)); def find(self, x): while self.parent[x] != x: x = self.parent[x]; return x; def union(self, x, y): rx, ry = self.find(x), self.find(y); if rx != ry: for i in range(len(self.parent)): if self.parent[i] == ry: self.parent[i] = rx.

examples.input: n=5, operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), find(0), find(3), union(1,2), find(0)
examples.output: After union(0,1): parent=[0,0,2,3,4]; After union(2,3): parent=[0,0,2,2,4]; find(0)=0; find(3)=2; After union(1,2): parent=[2,2,2,2,4]; find(0)=2
examples.trace: Initialize parent=[0,1,2,3,4]. Union(0,1): set parent[1]=0. Union(2,3): set parent[3]=2. Find(0)=0. Find(3)=2. Union(1,2): find(1)=0, find(2)=2, set all with parent=2 to 0. Find(0)=0.

examples.input: n=4, operations: union(0,2), union(1,3), find(2), union(0,3), find(1)
examples.output: After union(0,2): parent=[0,1,0,3]; After union(1,3): parent=[0,1,0,1]; find(2)=0; After union(0,3): parent=[1,1,1,1]; find(1)=1
examples.trace: Initialize parent=[0,1,2,3]. Union(0,2): set parent[2]=0. Union(1,3): set parent[3]=1. Find(2)=0. Union(0,3): find(0)=0, find(3)=1, set all with parent=1 to 0. Find(1)=0.

testCases.input: n=5, operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), union(0,2), find(3)
n=6, operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), union(4,5), union(0,2), union(0,4), find(5)
n=5, operations: union(0,1), union(1,2), union(2,3), union(3,4), find(4)
n=10, operations: union(0,9), union(1,8), union(2,7), union(3,6), union(4,5), union(0,1), union(2,3), union(4,0), union(6,8), find(7)

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Union / Find Operation
ladders.desc: Implement the union operation that merges two sets by updating all elements in one set to point to the other set's representative.
ladders.explanation: The union operation is the core of the naive array representation, responsible for merging two disjoint sets into one. The intuition is to connect all elements of one set to the root of another set, ensuring they share the same representative. This is done by first finding the representatives of both elements using the find operation, then if they are different, iterating through the entire array and updating all elements that belong to the second set to point to the first set's representative. Step-by-step: 1. Find the representative of x (call it rx). 2. Find the representative of y (call it ry). 3. If rx equals ry, the elements are already in the same set, so do nothing. 4. Otherwise, for each index i in the array: if parent[i] equals ry, set parent[i] to rx. This ensures all elements previously in ry's set now point to rx. Time: O(n) for the iteration, plus O(n) for find operations in worst-case, so O(n) overall. Space: O(1) extra space. This feeds into L0 by being the primary method for merging sets. Common mistakes include not checking if rx equals ry (leading to unnecessary updates), incorrect loop bounds, and misunderstanding the update condition. Real code pattern: def union(x, y): rx, ry = find(x), find(y); if rx != ry: for i in range(len(parent)): if parent[i] == ry: parent[i] = rx.

examples.trace: Initial parent=[0,1,2,3,4]. Find(0): parent[0]=0, rx=0. Find(2): parent[2]=2, ry=2. Since rx!=ry, iterate i from 0 to 4: i=0: parent[0]=0 !=2, no change; i=1: parent[1]=1 !=2, no change; i=2: parent[2]=2 ==2, set parent[2]=0; i=3: parent[3]=3 !=2, no change; i=4: parent[4]=4 !=2, no change. Final parent=[0,1,0,3,4].

Initial parent=[0,0,2,2,4]. Find(1): parent[1]=0, parent[0]=0, rx=0. Find(3): parent[3]=2, parent[2]=2, ry=2. Since rx!=ry, iterate i from 0 to 4: i=0: parent[0]=0 !=2, no change; i=1: parent[1]=0 !=2, no change; i=2: parent[2]=2 ==2, set parent[2]=0; i=3: parent[3]=2 ==2, set parent[3]=0; i=4: parent[4]=4 !=2, no change. Final parent=[0,0,0,0,4].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Merging Sets
ladders.desc: Implement the find operation that determines the representative of an element by following parent pointers until a self-loop is reached.
ladders.explanation: The find operation is central to union-find, as it identifies the representative (root) of a set for any given element. In the naive array representation, this is done by following parent pointers in the array until reaching an element that points to itself (a self-loop), which indicates the representative. The intuition is that each set forms a tree where the root is the representative, and by traversing up the tree via parent pointers, we can always find the root. Step-by-step: 1. Start with the given element x. 2. While parent[x] is not equal to x (i.e., not a self-loop), set x to parent[x]. 3. Return x as the representative. This process is guaranteed to terminate because the parent pointers eventually lead to a self-loop (initially, all elements are self-loops, and union operations maintain this property). Time: O(n) in worst-case if the tree becomes a long chain. Space: O(1). This feeds into L1 by providing the representatives needed for union operations. Common mistakes include infinite loops if self-loops are not handled correctly, off-by-one errors, and not updating x correctly in the loop. Real code pattern: def find(x): while parent[x] != x: x = parent[x]; return x.

examples.trace: Start with x=1. parent[1]=0, which is not equal to 1, so set x=0. parent[0]=0, which is equal to x (0), so loop ends. Return 0.
Start with x=3. parent[3]=3, which is equal to x (3), so loop ends immediately. Return 3.

ladders.title: Building Block — Parent Array Initialization
ladders.desc: Initialize an array to represent disjoint sets where each element is its own parent.
ladders.explanation: Initialization is the foundation of the naive array representation, setting up the data structure for union-find operations. The intuition is that initially, each element is in its own set, so it should point to itself as its parent, forming a self-loop. This is achieved by creating an array of size n, where the index represents the element and the value at that index represents its parent. By setting parent[i] = i for all i, we ensure that each element is its own representative. Step-by-step: 1. Create an array parent of size n. 2. For each index i from 0 to n-1, set parent[i] = i. This establishes n disjoint sets, each containing a single element. Time: O(n) to iterate through the array. Space: O(n) for the array. This feeds into L2 by providing the initial state for find operations. Common mistakes include using 1-based indexing incorrectly, not allocating enough space, or initializing with wrong values. Real code pattern: def initialize(n): parent = [i for i in range(n)]; return parent.

examples.trace: Create an array parent of size 3. For i=0: set parent[0]=0. For i=1: set parent[1]=1. For i=2: set parent[2]=2. Final parent=[0,1,2].
Create an array parent of size 5. For i=0: set parent[0]=0. For i=1: set parent[1]=1. For i=2: set parent[2]=2. For i=3: set parent[3]=3. For i=4: set parent[4]=4. Final parent=[0,1,2,3,4].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Access & Update
ladders.desc: Perform basic array operations: access an element by index and update its value.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves working with arrays, which are the underlying data structure for the naive union-find representation. The intuition is that arrays allow O(1) access and update by index, making them efficient for storing parent pointers. Accessing an element means retrieving the value at a given index, while updating means changing that value. Step-by-step: 1. To access: use array[index] to get the value. 2. To update: set array[index] = new_value. In union-find, this is used to read parent pointers during find and write new parent pointers during union. Time: O(1) for both access and update. Space: O(1) extra. This feeds into L3 by enabling the initialization and manipulation of the parent array. Common mistakes include index out-of-bounds errors, misunderstanding zero-based indexing, and incorrect assignment. Real code pattern: Access: val = arr[i]; Update: arr[i] = new_val.

examples.input: arr=[10,20,30], access index 1, update index 2 to 40
examples.trace: Initial arr=[10,20,30]. Access index 1: arr[1]=20. Update index 2: set arr[2]=40. New arr=[10,20,40].

examples.input: arr=[0,1,2,3], access index 0, update index 3 to 0
examples.trace: Initial arr=[0,1,2,3]. Access index 0: arr[0]=0. Update index 3: set arr[3]=0. New arr=[0,1,2,0].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Sets & Elements
ladders.desc: Basic understanding of sets, elements, and how to represent relationships using arrays.
ladders.explanation: A set is a collection of distinct elements. Initially, each element is in its own singleton set. We use an array where parent[i] = i means element i is its own representative. Union merges sets, find finds the representative.

examples.input: Create an array of size 3 with values 5, 10, 15. Access index 1.
examples.trace: Create array: arr = [5, 10, 15]. This has size 3, indices 0,1,2. Access index 1: arr[1] = 10.

examples.input: Create an array of size 4 initialized to zeros. Update index 2 to 7.
examples.trace: Create array: arr = [0, 0, 0, 0]. This has size 4. Update index 2: set arr[2] = 7. New array: [0, 0, 7, 0].

testCases.input: Create array of size 2 with values 8,9
Array: [7,8,9], what is index of value 8?
Create array of size 5 with all 1s
Array: [6,7,8], size after update index 1 to 9?

approaches.name: Union by Size/Rank + Path Compression
approaches.summary: Use two arrays: one for parent pointers and one for size/rank. Union operations connect smaller trees to larger ones to keep trees balanced. Path compression flattens the tree during find operations for efficiency.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Union
ladders.desc: Implement a full DSU class with union by size and path compression for efficient connectivity queries.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete implementation of the Disjoint Set Union (DSU) data structure using union by size and path compression. The intuition is to maintain a collection of disjoint sets where each set has a representative element, allowing efficient union and find operations. Union by size ensures that when merging two sets, we attach the smaller tree to the root of the larger tree, keeping the tree approximately balanced. Path compression flattens the tree during find operations by making every node on the path point directly to the root, reducing future find operation costs. The algorithm uses two arrays: parent[] to store parent pointers (with roots pointing to themselves) and size[] to track the number of elements in each set. The find(x) function recursively finds the root of x while applying path compression. The union(x, y) function finds the roots of x and y, and if they are different, attaches the smaller set's root to the larger set's root, updating the size. The time complexity is O(α(n)) amortized per operation, where α is the inverse Ackermann function (effectively constant), and space is O(n). This feeds into solving connectivity problems in graphs, dynamic connectivity, and component tracking. Common mistakes include not updating size correctly during union, forgetting path compression in find, and initializing parent incorrectly. Real code pattern: class DSU with __init__, find, union methods.

examples.input: Initialize DSU with n=5. Operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), find(0), union(1,2), find(3).
examples.output: After operations: parent=[0,0,0,0,4], size=[4,1,1,1,1]. find(0)=0, find(3)=0.
examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize parent=[0,1,2,3,4], size=[1,1,1,1,1]. Step 2: union(0,1): find(0)=0, find(1)=1, size[0]=1, size[1]=1, attach 1 to 0 (since sizes equal, choose 0 as root), parent[1]=0, size[0]=2, parent=[0,0,2,3,4], size=[2,1,1,1,1]. Step 3: union(2,3): find(2)=2, find(3)=3, size[2]=1, size[3]=1, attach 3 to 2, parent[3]=2, size[2]=2, parent=[0,0,2,2,4], size=[2,1,2,1,1]. Step 4: find(0): path from 0 to root 0, no compression, return 0. Step 5: union(1,2): find(1)=0 (after path compression: parent[1]=0), find(2)=2, size[0]=2, size[2]=2, attac
... (simplified)

examples.input: Initialize DSU with n=4. Operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), union(0,2), find(3).
examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize parent=[0,1,2,3], size=[1,1,1,1]. Step 2: union(0,1): find(0)=0, find(1)=1, size[0]=1, size[1]=1, attach 1 to 0, parent[1]=0, size[0]=2, parent=[0,0,2,3], size=[2,1,1,1]. Step 3: union(2,3): find(2)=2, find(3)=3, size[2]=1, size[3]=1, attach 3 to 2, parent[3]=2, size[2]=2, parent=[0,0,2,2], size=[2,1,2,1]. Step 4: union(0,2): find(0)=0, find(2)=2, size[0]=2, size[2]=2, attach 2 to 0, parent[2]=0, size[0]=4, parent=[0,0,0,2], size=[4,1,2,1]. Step 5: find(3): find(3) calls find(2) which returns 0, path compression sets parent[3
... (simplified)

testCases.input: n=4, operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), union(0,2), find(3)
n=5, operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), union(0,2), union(3,4), find(4)
n=6, operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), union(4,5), union(0,2), union(0,4), find(5)

n=5, operations: union(0,1), union(1,2), union(3,4), union(0,3), find(4)
n=10, operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), union(4,5), union(6,7), union(8,9), union(0,2), union(4,6), union(0,4), union(0,8), find(9)

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Union / Find Operation
ladders.desc: Implement the find function that locates the root of an element and applies path compression.
ladders.explanation: The find function is the core operation in DSU that determines the representative (root) of a set for a given element. The intuition is to recursively traverse parent pointers until reaching the root (where parent[x] == x), and during this traversal, apply path compression by setting each node's parent directly to the root. This flattens the tree, making future find operations faster. The algorithm: if parent[x] != x, then set parent[x] = find(parent[x]) (recursive call with path compression), and return parent[x]. This ensures that after the first find, all nodes on the path point directly to the root. Time complexity is O(α(n)) amortized due to path compression, and space is O(n) for recursion stack in worst case (can be iterative to avoid stack). This feeds into the full DSU by enabling efficient root finding for union and connectivity checks. Common mistakes include not using recursion or forgetting to update parent pointers, leading to no compression. Real code pattern: def find(x): if parent[x] != x: parent[x] = find(parent[x]); return parent[x].

examples.trace: Step 1: find(4): parent[4]=3 != 4, call find(3). Step 2: find(3): parent[3]=2 != 3, call find(2). Step 3: find(2): parent[2]=1 != 2, call find(1). Step 4: find(1): parent[1]=0 != 1, call find(0). Step 5: find(0): parent[0]=0, return 0. Step 6: Backtrack: set parent[1]=0, return 0. Step 7: Backtrack: set parent[2]=0, return 0. Step 8: Backtrack: set parent[3]=0, return 0. Step 9: Backtrack: set parent[4]=0, return 0. Final parent=[0,0,0,0,0].
Step 1: find(4): parent[4]=4, return 4. No compression needed as it's already root. Parent remains [0,1,2,3,4].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Merging Sets
ladders.desc: Implement the union operation that connects two sets by attaching the smaller tree to the larger tree's root.
ladders.explanation: The union operation merges two disjoint sets into one. The intuition behind union by size is to keep the tree balanced by always attaching the root of the smaller set to the root of the larger set. This minimizes the height of the resulting tree, which in turn reduces the time for future find operations. The algorithm: first, find the roots of elements x and y using the find function. If the roots are different, compare their sizes (stored in a size array). Attach the root of the smaller set to the root of the larger set by updating the parent pointer. Then, update the size of the new root to be the sum of the two sizes. If sizes are equal, either can be attached to the other, but we typically choose one consistently (e.g., attach y's root to x's root). Time complexity is O(α(n)) amortized due to the find operations, and space is O(1) extra. This feeds into the full DSU by enabling efficient set merging. Common mistakes include not updating size correctly, attaching larger to smaller, or forgetting to check if roots are already the same. Real code pattern: def union(x, y): rootX = find(x); rootY = find(y); if rootX != rootY: if size[rootX] < size[rootY]: swap; parent[rootY] = rootX; size[rootX] += size[rootY].

examples.trace: Step 1: find(0)=0, find(1)=1. Step 2: Roots different: 0 and 1. Step 3: size[0]=1, size[1]=1, equal so attach 1 to 0 (choose 0 as root). Step 4: parent[1]=0. Step 5: size[0] = 1+1 = 2. Final parent=[0,0,2,3], size=[2,1,1,1].

Step 1: find(1)=0, find(3)=2. Step 2: Roots different: 0 and 2. Step 3: size[0]=2, size[2]=2, equal so attach 2 to 0. Step 4: parent[2]=0. Step 5: size[0] = 2+2 = 4. Final parent=[0,0,0,2], size=[4,1,2,1].

ladders.title: Building Block — Parent Array Initialization
ladders.desc: Set up the parent array where each element initially points to itself, representing singleton sets.
ladders.explanation: The parent pointer array is fundamental to DSU, representing the forest of trees where each node points to its parent. The intuition is that initially, each element is in its own set, so it is the root of its tree, and thus points to itself. This initialization sets the stage for union and find operations. The algorithm: create an array parent of size n, and for each index i from 0 to n-1, set parent[i] = i. This ensures that find(i) will return i initially, and union operations will link these roots. Time complexity is O(n) for initialization, and space is O(n). This feeds into the core logic by providing the base structure for tracking set relationships. Common mistakes include initializing to 0 or -1, which breaks the self-pointer invariant. Real code pattern: parent = list(range(n)).

examples.trace: Step 1: Create array of size 3. Step 2: Set parent[0]=0. Step 3: Set parent[1]=1. Step 4: Set parent[2]=2. Final parent=[0,1,2].
Step 1: Create array of size 5. Step 2: For i in 0 to 4: parent[i]=i. Final parent=[0,1,2,3,4].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Access & Update
ladders.desc: Determine if two elements belong to the same set by comparing their roots.
ladders.explanation: This operation checks connectivity between two elements in a DSU. The intuition is that two elements are in the same set if and only if they have the same root representative. After initializing the parent array, we can use the find function to get the roots of both elements and compare them. If the roots are equal, the elements are connected; otherwise, they are in different sets. The algorithm: rootX = find(x); rootY = find(y); return rootX == rootY. This is efficient because find uses path compression. Time complexity is O(α(n)) amortized, and space is O(1) extra. This feeds into the building block by providing a simple query operation that relies on parent pointers. Common mistakes include comparing parent[x] and parent[y] directly without using find, which may give incorrect results if path compression hasn't been applied. Real code pattern: def connected(x, y): return find(x) == find(y).

examples.input: parent=[0,0,2,2], find(0)=0, find(1)=0, find(2)=2, find(3)=2. Check if 0 and 1 are in same set, and if 0 and 2 are in same set.
examples.trace: Step 1: For 0 and 1: find(0)=0, find(1)=0, compare 0==0 -> true. Step 2: For 0 and 2: find(0)=0, find(2)=2, compare 0==2 -> false.

examples.input: parent=[0,1,2,3], all elements are roots. Check if 0 and 3 are in same set.
examples.trace: Step 1: find(0)=0, find(3)=3, compare 0==3 -> false.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Sets & Elements
ladders.desc: Basic understanding of sets, elements, and how to represent relationships using arrays.
ladders.explanation: A set is a collection of distinct elements. Initially, each element is in its own singleton set. We use an array where parent[i] = i means element i is its own representative. Union merges sets, find finds the representative.

examples.input: Create an array for n=3 where each element points to itself.
examples.trace: Step 1: Start with an empty array. Step 2: For i=0, set arr[0]=0. Step 3: For i=1, set arr[1]=1. Step 4: For i=2, set arr[2]=2. Final arr=[0,1,2].

examples.input: Given arr=[0,1,2,3], explain what arr[2]=2 means.
examples.output: It means element 2's parent is 2, so it is a root pointing to itself.
examples.trace: Step 1: Array indices: 0,1,2,3. Step 2: Value at index 2 is 2. Step 3: This indicates that the element with index 2 has a parent of 2, which is itself, so it is a root.

approaches.name: Disjoint Set Forest (Lazy Union)
approaches.summary: Represent sets as trees where each node points to its parent. Union operations simply link the roots of two trees without immediate balancing. Find operations traverse up to the root, potentially creating tall trees.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(log n) average case, O(n) worst case

ladders.title: Full Problem — Disjoint
ladders.desc: Implement a full DSU class with lazy union operations for dynamic connectivity problems.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete implementation of the Disjoint Set Forest with Lazy Union approach. The intuition is to represent disjoint sets as trees where each element points to its parent, with lazy union operations that simply link roots without immediate balancing. This creates efficient average-case performance while accepting worst-case linear time. The algorithm maintains two arrays: parent[] to track parent relationships and rank[] (or size[]) to optionally optimize unions. The find operation traverses up the tree to find the root, while union operation finds roots of two elements and links them. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Initialize parent[i] = i for all elements (each in its own set). 2. Find(x): while parent[x] ≠ x, set x = parent[x]; return x. 3. Union(x, y): find roots rx and ry; if rx ≠ ry, set parent[rx] = ry. Time complexity is O(log n) average case due to random linking creating balanced trees on average, but O(n) worst case when trees become chains. Space is O(n) for storing parent pointers. This feeds into solving connectivity problems like network connections, image segmentation, and Kruskal's algorithm. Common mistakes include not handling find recursion properly (causing stack overflow), forgetting to check if elements are already in same set before union, and not updating parent pointers correctly. Real code pattern: class DSU: def __init__(self, n): self.parent = list(range(n)); def find(self, x): while self.parent[x] != x: x = self.parent[x]; return x; def union(self, x, y): rx, ry = self.find(x), self.find(y); if rx != ry: self.parent[rx] = ry.

examples.input: Initialize DSU(5), union(0,1), union(2,3), union(0,2), find(4)
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize parent = [0,1,2,3,4]. Step 2: union(0,1): find(0)=0, find(1)=1, set parent[0]=1 → parent=[1,1,2,3,4]. Step 3: union(2,3): find(2)=2, find(3)=3, set parent[2]=3 → parent=[1,1,3,3,4]. Step 4: union(0,2): find(0)=1 (path: 0→1), find(2)=3 (path: 2→3), set parent[1]=3 → parent=[1,3,3,3,4]. Step 5: find(4)=4. Final roots: element 0 root=3 (0→1→3), 1 root=3, 2 root=3, 3 root=3, 4 root=4.

examples.input: Initialize DSU(4), union(0,1), union(1,2), union(2,3), find(0)
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize parent = [0,1,2,3]. Step 2: union(0,1): find(0)=0, find(1)=1, set parent[0]=1 → parent=[1,1,2,3]. Step 3: union(1,2): find(1)=1, find(2)=2, set parent[1]=2 → parent=[1,2,2,3]. Step 4: union(2,3): find(2)=2, find(3)=3, set parent[2]=3 → parent=[1,2,3,3]. Step 5: find(0): path 0→1→2→3, return 3. This shows worst-case chain formation.

testCases.input: DSU(6), union(0,1), union(2,3), union(4,5), union(0,2), union(2,4), find(5)
DSU(5), union(0,1), union(1,2), union(2,3), union(3,4), find(0)

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Union / Find Operation
ladders.desc: Implement the find operation that locates the root of an element's set.
ladders.explanation: The find operation is the most critical sub-routine in DSU, responsible for determining which set an element belongs to by finding its root. The intuition is that each set is represented as a tree, and the root serves as the unique identifier for that set. By traversing parent pointers upward until reaching a node that points to itself (the root), we can identify membership. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Start at element x. 2. While parent[x] ≠ x, set x = parent[x]. 3. Return x as the root. Time complexity is O(h) where h is the height of the tree, averaging O(log n) but worst-case O(n) in unbalanced trees. Space is O(1) aside from input storage. This feeds into the full DSU by enabling union operations to compare roots and find operations to answer connectivity queries. Common mistakes include infinite loops if parent pointers form cycles, not handling recursive implementations carefully (risk of stack overflow for deep trees), and confusing root with immediate parent. Real code pattern: def find(x): while parent[x] != x: x = parent[x]; return x.

examples.trace: Step 1: x=0, parent[0]=1 ≠ 0 → x=1. Step 2: x=1, parent[1]=2 ≠ 1 → x=2. Step 3: x=2, parent[2]=2 equals x → return 2. Path: 0→1→2.

Step 1: x=4, parent[4]=3 ≠ 4 → x=3. Step 2: x=3, parent[3]=2 ≠ 3 → x=2. Step 3: x=2, parent[2]=1 ≠ 2 → x=1. Step 4: x=1, parent[1]=0 ≠ 1 → x=0. Step 5: x=0, parent[0]=0 equals x → return 0. Path: 4→3→2→1→0.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Merging Sets
ladders.desc: Implement the union operation that merges two sets by linking their roots.
ladders.explanation: The union operation is the core logic that merges two disjoint sets into one. The intuition is that by making one root point to another, we combine their trees without immediately rebalancing, hence 'lazy'. This maintains the invariant that all elements in a set share the same root. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Find the roots of elements x and y using find operations. 2. If the roots are different (they belong to different sets), set parent[root_x] = root_y (or vice versa). 3. If roots are the same, do nothing (elements already in same set). Time complexity is dominated by the two find operations, so O(log n) average, O(n) worst. Space is O(1). This feeds into building the full DSU by enabling dynamic connectivity updates. Common mistakes include linking in the wrong direction (creating deeper trees), not checking if roots are already equal (unnecessary work), and modifying parent pointers incorrectly. Real code pattern: def union(x, y): rx, ry = find(x), find(y); if rx != ry: parent[rx] = ry.

examples.input: parent=[0,1,2,3], union(0,1)
examples.trace: Step 1: find(0)=0, find(1)=1. Step 2: roots 0 and 1 are different. Step 3: set parent[0]=1. Result: parent array becomes [1,1,2,3].

examples.input: parent=[1,1,2,3], union(0,2)
examples.trace: Step 1: find(0): 0→1, return 1; find(2)=2. Step 2: roots 1 and 2 are different. Step 3: set parent[1]=2. Result: parent becomes [1,2,2,3].

ladders.title: Building Block — Parent Array Initialization
ladders.desc: Create and initialize the parent array where each element points to itself.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves setting up the fundamental data structure for DSU: an array where each index represents an element, and its value represents its parent in the tree. The intuition is that initially, each element is in its own set, so it should be its own parent (root). This establishes the base state for all operations. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Create an array parent of size n. 2. For i from 0 to n-1, set parent[i] = i. Time complexity is O(n) for initialization. Space is O(n) for the array. This feeds into the DSU by providing the starting point for find and union operations. Common mistakes include using 1-based indexing incorrectly, not allocating enough memory, or initializing to wrong values (like all zeros). Real code pattern: parent = list(range(n)) or for i in range(n): parent[i] = i.

examples.input: Initialize for n=3
examples.trace: Step 1: Create array of size 3. Step 2: Set parent[0]=0, parent[1]=1, parent[2]=2. Result: [0,1,2].

examples.input: Initialize for n=5
examples.trace: Step 1: Create array of size 5. Step 2: Set parent[0]=0, parent[1]=1, parent[2]=2, parent[3]=3, parent[4]=4. Result: [0,1,2,3,4].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Access & Update
ladders.desc: Access and modify elements in an array using indices.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves working with arrays: reading values at specific indices and updating them. The intuition is that arrays provide direct access to elements via their positions, which is essential for implementing parent pointers in DSU. The step-by-step process: 1. To read: value = array[index]. 2. To write: array[index] = new_value. Time complexity is O(1) for both operations. Space is O(1) aside from the array itself. This feeds into DSU by enabling the manipulation of parent pointers during find and union. Common mistakes include index out-of-bounds errors, confusing indices with values, and not updating the correct index. Real code pattern: In Python, arr[i] accesses element, arr[i] = val modifies it.

examples.input: arr=[10,20,30], read index 1, write index 0 with 5
examples.trace: Step 1: Read arr[1] = 20. Step 2: Write arr[0] = 5. Result: arr is now [5,20,30].

examples.input: arr=[0,1,2,3], read index 3, write index 2 with 9
examples.trace: Step 1: Read arr[3] = 3. Step 2: Write arr[2] = 9. Result: arr is now [0,1,9,3].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Sets & Elements
ladders.desc: Basic understanding of sets, elements, and how to represent relationships using arrays.
ladders.explanation: A set is a collection of distinct elements. Initially, each element is in its own singleton set. We use an array where parent[i] = i means element i is its own representative. Union merges sets, find finds the representative.

examples.input: Elements: {0}, {1}, {2}. Union {0} and {1}.
examples.trace: Step 1: Start with three singleton sets: {0}, {1}, {2}. Step 2: Union {0} and {1} combines them into one set {0,1}. Result: two sets: {0,1} and {2}.

examples.input: Elements: {a}, {b}, {c}. Find if a and c are in same set.
examples.trace: Step 1: Sets are {a}, {b}, {c}. Step 2: Check if a and c are in same set: a is in {a}, c is in {c}, different sets. Result: False.

testCases.input: Sets: {0,1}, {2}. Find if 0 and 2 same set.
Sets: {a,b}, {c}. Find if a and b same set.
Sets: {x}, {y}, {z}. Union {x} and {y}, then union with {z}.
Sets: {1,2}, {3,4}. Find if 2 and 4 same set.
Sets: {0}, {0}. (Invalid as sets disjoint, treat as same)
Sets: {5}, {6}, {7}. Union {5} and {6}, find if 6 and 7 same.
Sets: {10}, {20}, {30}. Union {10} and {30}, find if 20 and 30 same.
